  <h1 class="article-title">
          Let’s Make One of Those Fancy Scrolling Animations Used on Apple Product Pages      </h1>

      <div class="author-row">

    
          <img style="border-radius: 50%;" src="https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/cb41a1028d77019c656d8cb29b04df6a5cd5ff225cefe001d8754e449804d600?s=80&d=retro&r=pg">
    
    <div>
      
              
        <a class="author-name" href="https://css-tricks.com/author/jurnvanwissen/">
          Jurn van Wissen        </a>
      
        on
              <time datetime="">
          May 22, 2020        </time> 

    </div>

  
</div>
  
</header>
        <div class="article-sponsor">

        <div class="article-content">

          
          
<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Apple is well-known for the sleek animations on their product pages. For example, as you scroll down the page products may slide into view, MacBooks fold open and iPhones spin, all while showing off the hardware, demonstrating the software and telling interactive stories of how the products are used.</p>



<span id="more-308477"></span>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Just check out this video of the mobile web experience for the iPad Pro:</p>



<figure class="wp-block-video ticss-96083e07"><video controls muted src="https://res.cloudinary.com/css-tricks/video/upload/f_auto,q_auto/v1588108817/ipad-pro_wdpam6.mp4" playsinline></video><figcaption>Source: <a href="https://twitter.com/saradietschy/status/1240281202055163906" rel="noopener">Twitter</a></figcaption></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">A lot of the effects that you see there aren’t created in just HTML and CSS. What then, you ask? Well, it can be a little hard to figure out. Even using the browser’s DevTools won’t always reveal the answer, as it often can’t see past a <code><canvas></code> element.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Let’s take an in-depth look at one of these effects to see how it’s made so you can recreate some of these magical effects in our own projects. Specifically, let’s replicate the <a href="https://www.apple.com/airpods-pro/" rel="noopener">AirPods Pro product page</a> and the shifting light effect in the hero image.</p>



<figure class="wp-block-video"><video controls src="https://res.cloudinary.com/css-tricks/video/upload/v1588889054/airpods-animation_g2qstg.mp4"></video></figure>



<h3 class="wp-block-heading" id="the-basic-concept"><a href="#the-basic-concept" aria-hidden="true" class="aal_anchor" id="the-basic-concept"><svg aria-hidden="true" class="aal_svg" height="16" version="1.1" viewBox="0 0 16 16" width="16" /></a>The basic concept</h3>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The idea is to create an animation just like a sequence of images in rapid succession. You know, like a flip book! No complex WebGL scenes or advanced JavaScript libraries are needed.</p>



<figure class="wp-block-video"><video controls src="https://res.cloudinary.com/css-tricks/video/upload/v1588889054/flipbook_tisnxf.mp4"></video></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">By synchronizing each frame to the user’s scroll position, we can play the animation as the user scrolls down (or back up) the page.</p>



<h3 class="wp-block-heading" id="start-with-the-markup-and-styles"><a href="#start-with-the-markup-and-styles" aria-hidden="true" class="aal_anchor" id="start-with-the-markup-and-styles"><svg aria-hidden="true" class="aal_svg" height="16" version="1.1" viewBox="0 0 16 16" width="16" /></a>Start with the markup and styles</h3>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The HTML and CSS for this effect is very easy as the magic happens inside the <code><canvas></code> element which we control with JavaScript by giving it an ID.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In CSS, we’ll give our document a height of 100vh and make our <code><body></code> 5⨉ taller than that to give ourselves the necessary scroll length to make this work. We’ll also match the background color of the document with the background color of our images.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The last thing we’ll do is position the <code><canvas></code>, center it, and limit the <code>max-width</code> and <code>height</code> so it does not exceed the dimensions of the viewport.</p>



<pre rel="CSS" class="wp-block-csstricks-code-block language-css" data-line=""><code markup="tt">html {
  height: 100vh;
}
 
body {
  background: #000;
  height: 500vh;
}
 
canvas {
  position: fixed;
  left: 50%;
  top: 50%;
  max-height: 100vh;
  max-width: 100vw;
  transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
}</code></pre>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Right now, we are able to scroll down the page (even though the content does not exceed the viewport height) and our <code><canvas></code> stays at the top of the viewport. That’s all the HTML and CSS we need.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Let’s move on to loading the images.</p>



<h3 class="wp-block-heading" id="fetching-the-correct-images"><a href="#fetching-the-correct-images" aria-hidden="true" class="aal_anchor" id="fetching-the-correct-images"><svg aria-hidden="true" class="aal_svg" height="16" version="1.1" viewBox="0 0 16 16" width="16" /></a>Fetching the correct images</h3>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Since we’ll be working with an image sequence (again, like a flip book), we’ll assume the file names are numbered sequentially in ascending order (i.e. 0001.jpg, 0002.jpg, 0003.jpg, etc.) in the same directory.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">We’ll write a function that returns the file path with the number of the image file we want, based off of the user’s scroll position.</p>



<pre rel="JavaScript" class="wp-block-csstricks-code-block language-javascript" data-line=""><code markup="tt">const currentFrame = index => (
  `https://www.apple.com/105/media/us/airpods-pro/2019/1299e2f5_9206_4470_b28e_08307a42f19b/anim/sequence/large/01-hero-lightpass/${index.toString().padStart(4, '0')}.jpg`
)</code></pre>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Since the image number is an integer, we’ll need to turn it in to a string and use <code>padStart(4, '0')</code> to prepend zeros in front of our index until we reach four digits to match our file names. So, for example, passing 1 into this function will return 0001.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">That gives us a way to handle image paths. Here’s the first image in the sequence drawn on the <code><canvas></code> element:</p>



<div class="wp-block-cp-codepen-gutenberg-embed-block cp_embed_wrapper"><iframe id="cp_embed_BaoQPeg" src="//codepen.io/anon/embed/BaoQPeg?height=450&theme-id=1&slug-hash=BaoQPeg&default-tab=result" height="450" scrolling="no" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen allowpaymentrequest name="CodePen Embed BaoQPeg" title="CodePen Embed BaoQPeg" class="cp_embed_iframe" style="width:100%;overflow:hidden">CodePen Embed Fallback</iframe></div>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">As you can see, the first image is on the page. At this point, it’s just a static file. What we want is to update it based on the user’s scroll position. And we don’t merely want to load one image file and then swap it out by loading another image file. We want to draw the images on the <code><canvas></code> and update the drawing with the next image in the sequence (but we’ll get to that in just a bit).</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">We already made the function to generate the image filepath based on the number we pass into it so what we need to do now is track the user’s scroll position and determine the corresponding image frame for that scroll position.</p>



<h3 class="wp-block-heading" id="connecting-images-to-the-users-scroll-progress"><a href="#connecting-images-to-the-users-scroll-progress" aria-hidden="true" class="aal_anchor" id="connecting-images-to-the-users-scroll-progress"><svg aria-hidden="true" class="aal_svg" height="16" version="1.1" viewBox="0 0 16 16" width="16" /></a>Connecting images to the user’s scroll progress</h3>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">To know which number we need to pass (and thus which image to load) in the sequence, we need to calculate the user’s scroll progress. We’ll make an event listener to track that and handle some math to calculate which image to load.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">We need to know:</p>



<ul class="wp-block-list"><li>Where scrolling starts and ends</li><li>The user’s scroll progress (i.e. a percentage of how far the user is down the page)</li><li>The image that corresponds to the user’s scroll progress</li></ul>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">We’ll use <a href="https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Element/scrollTop" rel="noopener"><code>scrollTop</code></a> to get the vertical scroll position of the element, which in our case happens to be the top of the document. That will serve as the starting point value. We’ll get the end (or maximum) value by subtracting the window height from the document scroll height. From there, we’ll divide the <code>scrollTop</code> value by the maximum value the user can scroll down, which gives us the user’s scroll progress.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Then we need to turn that scroll progress into an index number that corresponds with the image numbering sequence for us to return the correct image for that position. We can do this by multiplying the progress number by the number of frames (images) we have. We’ll use <code>Math.floor()</code> to round that number down and wrap it in <code>Math.min()</code> with our maximum frame count so it never exceeds the total number of frames.</p>



<pre rel="JavaScript" class="wp-block-csstricks-code-block language-javascript" data-line=""><code markup="tt">window.addEventListener('scroll', () => {  
  const scrollTop = html.scrollTop;
  const maxScrollTop = html.scrollHeight - window.innerHeight;
  const scrollFraction = scrollTop / maxScrollTop;
  const frameIndex = Math.min(
    frameCount - 1,
    Math.floor(scrollFraction * frameCount)
  );
});</code></pre>



<h3 class="wp-block-heading" id="updating-ltcanvasgt-with-the-correct-image"><a href="#updating-canvas-with-the-correct-image" aria-hidden="true" class="aal_anchor" id="updating-canvas-with-the-correct-image"><svg aria-hidden="true" class="aal_svg" height="16" version="1.1" viewBox="0 0 16 16" width="16" /></a>Updating <canvas> with the correct image</h3>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">We now know which image we need to draw as the user’s scroll progress changes. This is where the magic of  <canvas> comes into play. <code><canvas></code> has many cool features for building everything from <a href="https://css-tricks.com/learn-canvas-snake-game/">games</a> and animations to <a href="https://blog.jurn.io/launching-screely/" rel="noopener">design mockup generators</a> and everything in between!</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">One of those features is a method called <a href="https://css-tricks.com/using-requestanimationframe/"><code>requestAnimationFrame</code></a> that works with the browser to update <code><canvas></code> in a way we couldn’t do if we were working with straight image files instead. This is why I went with a <code><canvas></code> approach instead of, say, an <code><img></code> element or a <code><div></code> with a background image.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><code>requestAnimationFrame</code> will match the browser refresh rate and enable hardware acceleration by using WebGL to render it using the device’s video card or integrated graphics. In other words, we’ll get super smooth transitions between frames — no image flashes!</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Let’s call this function in our scroll event listener to swap images as the user scrolls up or down the page. <code>requestAnimationFrame</code> takes a callback argument, so we’ll pass a function that will update the image source and draw the new image on the <code><canvas></code>:</p>



<pre rel="JavaScript" class="wp-block-csstricks-code-block language-javascript" data-line=""><code markup="tt">requestAnimationFrame(() => updateImage(frameIndex + 1))</code></pre>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">We’re bumping up the <code>frameIndex</code> by 1 because, while the image sequence starts at 0001.jpg, our scroll progress calculation starts actually starts at 0. This ensures that the two values are always aligned.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The callback function we pass to update the image looks like this:</p>



<pre rel="JavaScript" class="wp-block-csstricks-code-block language-javascript" data-line=""><code markup="tt">const updateImage = index => {
  img.src = currentFrame(index);
  context.drawImage(img, 0, 0);
}</code></pre>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">We pass the <code>frameIndex</code> into the function. That sets the image source with the next image in the sequence, which is drawn on our <code><canvas></code> element.</p>



<h3 class="wp-block-heading" id="even-better-with-image-preloading"><a href="#even-better-with-image-preloading" aria-hidden="true" class="aal_anchor" id="even-better-with-image-preloading"><svg aria-hidden="true" class="aal_svg" height="16" version="1.1" viewBox="0 0 16 16" width="16" /></a>Even better with image preloading</h3>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">We’re technically done at this point. But, come on, we can do better! For example, scrolling quickly results in a little lag between image frames. That’s because every new image sends off a new network request, requiring a new download.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">We should try preloading the images new network requests. That way, each frame is already downloaded, making the transitions that much faster, and the animation that much smoother!</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">All we’ve gotta do is loop through the entire sequence of images and load ‘em up:</p>



<pre rel="JavaScript" class="wp-block-csstricks-code-block language-javascript" data-line=""><code markup="tt">const frameCount = 148;
 
const preloadImages = () => {
  for (let i = 1; i < frameCount; i++) {
    const img = new Image();
    img.src = currentFrame(i);
  }
};
 
preloadImages();</code></pre>



<h3 class="wp-block-heading" id="demo"><a href="#demo" aria-hidden="true" class="aal_anchor" id="demo"><svg aria-hidden="true" class="aal_svg" height="16" version="1.1" viewBox="0 0 16 16" width="16" /></a>Demo!</h3>



<div class="wp-block-cp-codepen-gutenberg-embed-block cp_embed_wrapper"><iframe id="cp_embed_ZEbGzyv" src="//codepen.io/anon/embed/ZEbGzyv?height=450&theme-id=1&slug-hash=ZEbGzyv&default-tab=result" height="450" scrolling="no" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen allowpaymentrequest name="CodePen Embed ZEbGzyv" title="CodePen Embed ZEbGzyv" class="cp_embed_iframe" style="width:100%;overflow:hidden">CodePen Embed Fallback</iframe></div>



<h3 class="wp-block-heading" id="a-quick-note-on-performance"><a href="#a-quick-note-on-performance" aria-hidden="true" class="aal_anchor" id="a-quick-note-on-performance"><svg aria-hidden="true" class="aal_svg" height="16" version="1.1" viewBox="0 0 16 16" width="16" /></a>A quick note on performance</h3>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">While this effect is pretty slick, it’s also <em>a lot of images.</em> 148 to be exact.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">No matter much we optimize the images, or how speedy the CDN is that serves them, loading hundreds of images will always result in a bloated page. Let’s say we have <em>multiple</em> instances of this on the same page. We might get performance stats like this:</p>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img fetchpriority="high" decoding="async" width="1440" height="56" src="https://i2.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/rgnzixsg.png?fit=1024%2C40&ssl=1" alt="1,609 requests, 55.8 megabytes transferred, 57.5 megabytes resources, load time of 30.45 seconds." class="wp-image-308480" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/rgnzixsg.png?w=1440&ssl=1 1440w, https://i0.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/rgnzixsg.png?resize=300%2C12&ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/rgnzixsg.png?resize=1024%2C40&ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/rgnzixsg.png?resize=768%2C30&ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/rgnzixsg.png?resize=1000%2C39&ssl=1 1000w" sizes="(min-width: 735px) 864px, 96vw"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">That might be fine for a high-speed internet connection without tight data caps, but we can’t say the same for users without such luxuries. It’s a tricky balance to strike, but we have to be mindful of everyone’s experience — and how our decisions affect them.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">A few things we can do to help strike that balance include:</p>



<ul class="wp-block-list"><li>Loading a single fallback image instead of the entire image sequence</li><li>Creating sequences that use smaller image files for certain devices</li><li>Allowing the user to enable the sequence, perhaps with a button that starts and stops the sequence</li></ul>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Apple employs the first option. If you load the <a href="https://www.apple.com/airpods-pro/" rel="noopener">AirPods Pro page</a> on a mobile device connected to a slow 3G connection and, hey, the performance stats start to look a whole lot better:</p>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img decoding="async" width="1636" height="56" src="https://i1.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/kJPcmdlA.png?fit=1024%2C35&ssl=1" alt="8 out of 111 requests, 347 kilobytes of 2.6 megabytes transferred, 1.4 megabytes of 4.5 megabytes resources, load time of one minute and one second." class="wp-image-308481" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/kJPcmdlA.png?w=1636&ssl=1 1636w, https://i0.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/kJPcmdlA.png?resize=300%2C10&ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/kJPcmdlA.png?resize=1024%2C35&ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/kJPcmdlA.png?resize=768%2C26&ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/kJPcmdlA.png?resize=1536%2C53&ssl=1 1536w, https://i0.wp.com/css-tricks.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/kJPcmdlA.png?resize=1000%2C34&ssl=1 1000w" sizes="(min-width: 735px) 864px, 96vw"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Yeah, it’s still a heavy page. But it’s a lot lighter than what we’d get without any performance considerations at all. That’s how Apple is able to get get so many complex sequences onto a single page.</p>



<hr class="wp-block-separator">



<h4 class="wp-block-heading" id="further-reading"><a href="#further-reading" aria-hidden="true" class="aal_anchor" id="further-reading"><svg aria-hidden="true" class="aal_svg" height="16" version="1.1" viewBox="0 0 16 16" width="16" /></a>Further reading</h4>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">If you are interested in how these image sequences are generated, a good place to start is the <a href="https://airbnb.design/lottie/" rel="noopener">Lottie library</a> by AirBnB. The docs take you through the basics of generating animations with After Effects while providing an easy way to include them in projects.</p>

        <div class="comment-author">Jurn van Wissen</div> <div class="comment-time"><a href="https://css-tricks.com/lets-make-one-of-those-fancy-scrolling-animations-used-on-apple-product-pages/#comment-1756973"><span class="screen-reader">Permalink to comment</span>#</a> <time datetime="2020-05-22T21:56:04-06:00">May 22, 2020</time></div>
      </div>

      
      <div class="comment-content">

        <p>It’s certainly possible to use a single video file and synchronize that to the user’s scroll position. I suspect download performance is roughly similar. Since I don’t have Apple’s source file I can’t do an A/B test to see which one has the bigger total file size, 148 images or a single video. Zahar is right in pointing out that it would decrease the number of http requests made but with HTTP/2 these can all be done one a single TCP connection so I suspect there is not much performance to be gained there.</p>
<p>The most important downside to video, as Malte demonstrated, is lag. The video html tag can not utilize <code>requestAnimationFrame()</code> which is the most important factor in making this effect as smooth as possible. requestAnimationFrame uses your video card to render it instead of the CPU and is limited to 60fps by the browser so your machine can keep up.</p>

      </div>

  <li class="comment even depth-2" id="li-comment-1756979">

    <div class="comment-wrap" id="comment-1756979">

        <div class="comment-author">Abhishek Ghosh</div> <div class="comment-time"><a href="https://css-tricks.com/lets-make-one-of-those-fancy-scrolling-animations-used-on-apple-product-pages/#comment-1756979"><span class="screen-reader">Permalink to comment</span>#</a> <time datetime="2020-05-22T23:58:28-06:00">May 22, 2020</time></div>
      </div>

      
      <div class="comment-content">

        <p>In the past, I had tried a bunch of approaches on the same, and some of them work better than others. In summary, a direct video frame-seek wouldn’t work as efficiently and smoothly with scroll as you’d imagine (frame drops happen; browsers internally try to optimise avoiding repeated work) especially on mobile devices. However, you could still find multiple nifty ways to unpack a video into frames directly within the browser and use them to create your scrolling animation. Videos generally compresses better, but distinct images demand much less compute work on the browser, and with careful sequencing and preloading, it’s possible that you can get the animation sequence started much faster than a video unpack. Aspects of image quality and total size really can depend on the fidelity of the animation you’re gunning for (no. of frames, resolution).</p>
<p>I hope you find this article interesting (includes demos and source code): <a href="https://www.ghosh.dev/posts/playing-with-video-scrubbing-animations-on-the-web/" rel="nofollow ugc">https://www.ghosh.dev/posts/playing-with-video-scrubbing-animations-on-the-web/</a></p>

      </div>

  <li class="comment odd alt depth-2" id="li-comment-1757010">

    <div class="comment-wrap" id="comment-1757010">

      <div class="comment-author-wrap">
        <div class="comment-author">Malte Janßen</div> <div class="comment-time"><a href="https://css-tricks.com/lets-make-one-of-those-fancy-scrolling-animations-used-on-apple-product-pages/#comment-1757010"><span class="screen-reader">Permalink to comment</span>#</a> <time datetime="2020-05-24T01:53:26-06:00">May 24, 2020</time></div>
      </div>

      
      <div class="comment-content">

        <p>To test the size difference, I generated images and videos myself using blender, and the results are pretty clear: 90 images take up 56MB, while a three second video with 30fps takes only 1.92MB.</p>
<p>Contrary to what Jurn van Wissen stated, requestAnimationFrame() does not decide what get’s accelerated by the GPU or not, that’s up to the browser. But it makes updates that should happen every frame “smoother”, because it only get’s called before every repaint, thus saving processing power by not update more often than needed.<br>
This is of course used by the video element too, we don’t need to set it’s currentTime more than every frame.</p>
<p>What I think is causing the lag is the interpolation between the video keyframes. As you can see, the upper two videos, rendered with a keyframe interval of 1 are way smoother than the bottom one(*), rendered with a keyframe interval of 20.</p>
<p>At least on my pc. On my phone, all of the videos stuttered noticeably, with the VP9/WEBM video performing the best.</p>

      </div>

        <p>Absolutely!<br>

  <li class="comment byuser comment-author-jonathanland odd alt thread-even depth-1" id="li-comment-1757606">

    <div class="comment-wrap" id="comment-1757606">

      <div class="comment-author-wrap">
        <div class="comment-author">Jonathan Land</div> <div class="comment-time"><a href="https://css-tricks.com/lets-make-one-of-those-fancy-scrolling-animations-used-on-apple-product-pages/#comment-1757606"><span class="screen-reader">Permalink to comment</span>#</a> <time datetime="2020-06-10T06:40:18-06:00">June 10, 2020</time></div>
      </div>

      
      <div class="comment-content">

        <p>Thanks a lot for the article, this is really a great technique and you’ve made it very clear how to implement.</p>
<p>One small but significant issue I’ve found is that setting <code>img.src = currentFrame(index)</code> inside <code>updateImage</code> makes a new network request each time, ignoring the browser cache (at least in Chrome).</p>
<p>My solution is to put the <code>img</code>s in an array when preloading, and then draw the image from the cache:</p>
<pre><code class="language-js">const images = [null] // since everything else is 1-indexed, explicitly fill images[0]
const preloadImages = () => {
  for (let i = 1; i < frameCount; i++) {
    const images[i] = new Image();
    images[i].src = currentFrame(i);
  }
};
//...
const updateImage = index => {
  context.drawImage(images[index], 0, 0); 
  /* 
     instead of:
     img.src = currentFrame(index); 
     context.drawImage(img, 0, 0); 
  */
}
</code></pre>

      </div>

      <div class="comment-actions">
        <div class="reply">
                  </div>
      </div>

    </div>

    
  
</li><!-- #comment-## -->
